title: Firm Investment and Stakeholder Choices: A Top-Down Theory of Capital Budgeting
abstract: This paper develops a top-down model of capital budgeting in which privately informed executives make investment choices that convey information to the firm's stakeholders (e.g., employees). Favorable information in this setting encourages stakeholders to take actions that positively contribute to the firm's success (e.g., employees work harder). Within this framework we examine how firms may distort their investment choices to influence the information conveyed to stakeholders and show that investment rigidities and overinvestment can arise as optimal investment distortions. We also examine investment distortions in multi-divisional firms and compare such distortions to those in single-division firms.

article.introduction:
This paper develops atop-down model of capital allocation in which privately informed executives make investment choices that convey information to the firm's stakeholders. The information conveyed by the executives' investment choices is relevant because it influences the actions of the stakeholders, which in turn affect the likelihood of the firm's success. Specifically, since higher investment expenditures are associated with better firm prospects, stakeholders, who receive a share of the output, are willing to expend more effort to improve the probability that the firm will be successful. Within this setting, we examine the optimal design of capital allocation mechanisms. We show that the process used to make investment choices affects how the information conveyed by investment choices is interpreted by stakeholders, which in turn influences how the capital budgeting process is designed.
To formalize these ideas, our model considers a firm whose production process requires a capital investment as well as effort exerted by a stakeholder (also referred to as the worker). In particular, we consider a framework in which the firm's owner (also referred to as the executive) obtains private information about the firm's prospects, which can be high or low, and chooses both the level of investment and the worker's compensation. In our framework, the worker's effort is more productive when the future prospects of the firm are better, which, in combination with the optimal compensation contract, induces the worker to exert more effort when his beliefs about the firm's future prospects are more favorable. As a result, the owner has an incentive to overinvest relative to the case in which he has no private information, because doing so conveys favorable information.
In this setting, there is a trade-off between the benefits of using the executive's private information to adjust its capital allocation and the inherent tendency to overinvest that arises from such discretionary capital allocation choices. As a result of this trade-off, two alternative capital allocation mechanisms emerge as potentially optimal: (i) a separating mechanism in which a high-prospect firm invests more (and offers higher compensation to the worker) than a low-prospect firm, and (ii) a pooling mechanism in which firms commit to a fixed level of investment and compensation. Under the separating mechanism, the firm invests more when the marginal productivity of capital is higher but tends to overinvest because of the potential benefits associated with conveying favorable information. Under the pooling mechanism, investment rigidities arise, that is, the level of investment is independent of the executive's information, and the firm's overinvestment tendency is suppressed. The choice between the pooling and the separating mechanisms is determined by a trade-off between the efficiency loss of a rigid investment policy that does not incorporate information and the cost associated with overinvestment.1
The information and incentive issues explored in this paper also have implications for how corporations are organized. To investigate this issue, we extend our analysis to consider the case of a two-division firm with top executives that have superior information about the prospects of each division. The capital investment choices of each of the divisions are observable and convey information. As we show, the optimal investment policy of a two-division firm generates (weakly) more value than the optimal investment policies of two independent firms acting independently. Intuitively, the gain from conglomeration is directly related to the incentives to distort investment that arise when executives have private information about the divisions' prospects. Although executives of a two-division firm have incentives to exaggerate the prospects of each of the divisions, they do not have an incentive to misrepresent the divisions' relative prospects. As a result, instead of overinvesting, firms can use asymmetric capital allocations to provide credible information about the relative prospects of the divisions.2Our analysis therefore provides a novel rationale for conglomerates, namely, the reduction in the cost of investment distortions that arise when a firm's stakeholders use the firm's investment choices to make inferences about the firm's prospects.
This paper addresses questions that have been previously considered in a number of threads of the literature. For instance, our analysis is related to papers in the leadership literature that examine how choices made by leaders influence individuals at lower levels in the organization (e.g., Rotemberg and Saloner (2000)). In particular, our analysis is closely related to Hermalin (1998) and Komai, Stegeman, and Hermalin (2007), who show that an informed leader signals his favorable information by expending greater effort, and in doing so motivates his subordinates to work harder (i.e., leadership effects). There are key differences, however, between our setting and the setting considered in these leadership papers. First, while these studies focus on how leadership effects can mitigate undereffort in teams, our analysis focuses on how the optimal design of the capital investment process can sometimes limit and other times take advantage of these leadership effects. Moreover, we consider a setting with contractible investment choices, which contrasts with leadership models that assume that the leader's effort is not contractible. Indeed, we find that a firm may find it optimal to commit to a rigid investment policy (i.e., a pooling mechanism) that does not fully incorporate top executives' information.
More broadly, our paper belongs to the principal-agent literature and specifically to the thread that considers the case of an informed principal that faces an agent with a moral hazard problem.3Among other things, this literature analyzes the effects of the principal's information on the optimal compensation contract (e.g., Beaudry (1994) and Inderst (2001)), the value of private information to the principal (e.g., Chade and Silvers (2002)), and the incentives to disclose information (i.e., provide “advice” ) to the agent (e.g., Strausz (2009)). While most of these papers (Strausz (2009) is an exception) consider the case of a principal who makes choices after becoming informed (i.e., signaling), we consider a mechanism design setting in which the principal commits to a self-screening mechanism prior to acquiring information and then, after becoming informed, makes choices that convey information that influences the agent's actions.4Within this self-screening paradigm we focus on the case in which the principal has limited ability to commit to make investment choices that are suboptimal given his private information. As we show, the principal's imperfect ability to commit implies that the optimal mechanism can be a pooling mechanism in which the principal conceals information from the agent and in which there are rigidities in the firm's investment choices.5
Our paper is also related to two other threads of the agency literature.6The first argues, that since managers get private benefits from managing larger enterprises, shareholders may want to impose restrictions on managers that inhibit their incentives to overinvest (e.g., Jensen (1986) and Hart and Moore (1995)). As we demonstrate, the tendency to overinvest, as well as mechanisms to curb this tendency, can arise in a setting with asymmetric information but without managerial private benefits. The second thread considers the investment misincentives that arise in conglomerates. This literature explores a tendency toward “corporate socialism”, what Scharfstein and Stein (2000) describe as a tendency “to engage in cross-subsidization, spending relatively too much in some divisions, and too little in others” (p. 2538). Our model also generates capital misallocation in conglomerates.39However, what would be a misallocation of capital under symmetric information arises in our model as an optimal response to the information problems that firms face with their stakeholders. Moreover, we find that capital misallocation within a conglomerate tends to be less costly than the capital misallocation that arises if divisions are operated as independent single-division firms.
We are not, however, the first to consider how capital allocations can be improved by conglomerates. Starting with Williamson (1975), researchers have considered the costs and benefits of external versus internal capital markets. The papers in this literature, such as Stein (1997), stress the beneficial role of internal capital markets as winner-pickers in settings with credit constraints and managerial agency conflicts. As we show, information asymmetry between firms and their stakeholders provides an additional channel that can contribute to the benefits of internal capital markets.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionI.presents the base model and SectionII.analyzes it. SectionIII.extends the base model by considering the case of multi-divisional firms. SectionIV.considers several additional extensions of our analysis and SectionV.discusses empirical implications suggested by our results. Finally, SectionVI.concludes. Proofs and other technical derivations are relegated to the Appendix.
article.I. The Model:
We consider a firm that operates in a risk-neutral economy. The firm is owned and run by an executive (“the principal”) and requires input from a worker (“the agent”) who is penniless, is subject to limited liability, and has a reservation wage of zero.
We consider the above setting for economic as well as technical reasons. First, it allows us to derive optimal compensation contracts and thus to analyze the interaction between optimal compensation and optimal investment in a relatively straightforward manner. Second, by assuming a complementarity between effort and the productivity shock (i.e., the probability of being successful is the product ofeand θ), this setting captures the insight that inducing effort is more efficient when prospects are more favorable.7Finally, the above setting implies decreasing returns to scale, since (i) firm revenue increases linearly with firm scale while costs are quadratic in firm scale and (ii) the agent experiences a quadratic cost of effort that increases linearly with firm scale. Taken together, these assumptions imply that there is an interior solution to the choice of investment and effort (i.e., due to the increasing marginal costs of effort and scale) and that the worker's moral hazard problem is independent of the firm's scale.8
In what follows, we restrict the analysis to mechanisms that consider compensation contracts that offer the agent a nonnegative paymentw(which can be contingent on the firm's scalek) when realized output is high, that is, whenq=rk, and a payment of zero when output is low, that is, whenq=0.9Thus, the principal's choice set can be described as a menu ofninvestment-compensation pairs,{(ki,wi)}i=1,…,n, which without loss of generality can be restricted ton=2, that is, mechanisms that contain at most two distinct pairs,{(ka,wa),(kb,wb)}.
The timing of events is as follows. Att=0, before observing θ, the principal specifies a menu of two investment (firm scale) and compensation (performance bonus) pairs,{(ka,wa),(kb,wb)}. Att=1, the principal privately observes θ and chooses one of the pairs, that is, either(ka,wa)or(kb,wb), from the pre-specified menu. Att=2, the agent makes an unobservable effort choicee. Att=3, firm outputq∼is realized and contracts are settled.
In short, we analyze the model as a mechanism design problem in which the optimal mechanism is determined before the principal becomes informed. It is worth mentioning that the design of the optimal mechanism accounts for the effect of the principal's choices on the agent's effort, but, since effort is unobservable, it excludes mechanisms with contracts that specify the agent's effort. Figure1summarizes the timing of events.
article.II. Model Analysis:
For each realization of the shock,θ={1,β}, the principal maximizes firm value subject to the corresponding agent's optimal effort choice6and participation constraint7. The problem can be simplified because the agent's limited liability requireswθ≥0and, sincee=0is feasible, the constraints included in7always hold.10

Proposition1indicates that, in the optimal mechanismM*, compensation, investment, and effort increase in θ, that is,wβ*>w1*,kβ*>k1*, andeβ*>e1*. Intuitively,M*implies higher investment and greater effort in state β because the marginal products of capital and effort are increasing in θ. Notice that, inM*, compensation is a sharing rule independent of θ (i.e.,wθ*rkθ*=12), that is, the worker receives half of total output independent of the firm's productivity θ.
In this section we examine the case in which the principal but not the agent observes the shock θ. In this case the mechanism design problem must consider how the principal's choices convey information about θ and how the information conveyed interacts with the agent's incentives.
In what follows we analyze the mechanism design problem faced by an uninformed principal att=0who anticipates that he will become informed about θ att=1. In particular, att=0the principal imposes a menu that limits his own potential choices att=1with the objective to influence the transmission of information that occurs when he actually makes a choice after learning θ.11We assume that these imposed limits are enforceable (i.e., the executive can indeed commit to limit his choices att=1to those specified in the menu att=0).12
While the uninformed principal can limit himself to choose from a pre-specified menu of choices, we assume that att=1the informed principal makes the optimal choice (conditional on the observed θ) among those available in the pre-specified menu.13In other words, the principal has limited ability to commit att=1. This limited ability to commit plays an important role in the mechanism design problem under consideration. In particular, the principal's limited ability to commit excludes mechanisms in which the principal's choices are delegated to a third party who may subsequently force suboptimal choices on the principal. Our focus on mechanisms in which the principal cannot be forced to make suboptimal investment and compensation decisions is appropriate in a setting like ours, which is designed to capture the interaction between executives and lower-level managers in the same firm.
The combination of the above assumptions on timing and (limited) commitment simplifies the procedure to solve for the optimal mechanism. To understand why this is the case, consider an alternative setting in which the mechanism designer (i.e., the principal) has full ability to commit but faces an agent subject to moral hazard. With full commitment ability, the optimal mechanism implied by the revelation principle can be a mediated mechanism in which the principal discloses his private information to a third party (i.e., a mediator) who may partially reveal the information to the agent while forcing the principal to make choices that are potentially suboptimal for the principal.14
In a setting like ours, however, in which the principal cannot commit to suboptimal choices, the search for the optimal mechanism can be limited to the class of nonmediated mechanisms, that is mechanisms in which there is no mediator but rather direct communication between the principal and the agent. In this case, the optimal mechanism can be derived by solving for the optimal menu of self-imposed constraints att=0, while imposing the screening (i.e., incentive compatibility) constraints on the choice att=1. As it turns out, the optimal mechanism does not necessarily imply full disclosure of the principal's information to the agent either directly by publicly revealing it or indirectly by making choices that lead the agent to learn the principal's information (i.e., by implementing actions that depend on the observed θ). In other words, in our setting with limited commitment by the principal, and with the agent's moral hazard, the revelation principle does not apply.15
Formally, in our setting the optimal mechanism can be derived by considering a pre-specified menu of at most two choices for the uninformed principal{(ka,wa),(kb,wb)}from which the informed principal optimally chooses(ka,wa)or(kb,wb).16Therefore, three types of mechanisms can potentially emerge as optimal: (i) a separating mechanismMsin which the principal's choices depend on, and thus reveal, the observed θ (i.e., the principal chooses(k1,w1)after observingθ=1and(kβ,wβ), wherek1≠kβorw1≠wβ, after observingθ=β), (ii) a pooling mechanismMpin which the principal's choices do not depend on the observed θ and thus do not reveal θ, that is,k1=kβandw1=wβ, and (iii) a partial pooling mechanismMppin which the principal mixes between two investment-compensation pairs and thus partially reveals θ to the agent.17
In what follows, we solve for the optimal mechanism within the separating and pooling classes and compare firm values when the optimal mechanisms within these classes are implemented. As shown in the Appendix, this is without loss of generality since firm value under partial pooling is lower than firm value when either the optimal separating mechanism or the optimal pooling mechanism is considered.18

A comparison of Propositions1and2reveals that, relative to the case in which θ is observable, compensation and investment remain unchanged for the low-prospect firm,(k1s*,w1s*)=(k1*,w1*), and increase by the factor Δ for the high-prospect firm,(kβs*,wβs*)=(Δkβ*,Δwβ*). Effort turns out to be equal to the effort exerted when θ is observable (i.e.,eθ*=eθs*). This is because the level of effort, which is proportional to the ratio of compensation to investment, is the same whether or not θ is observable (i.e.,eθ*=wθs*θckθs*=wθ*θckθ*).21
The overinvestment factor Δ captures the essence of the analysis in the optimal separating mechanism. The high-prospect firm overinvests (i.e., increases its scale relative to the case of observable θ) to credibly communicate to the agent the firm's high prospects. As Proposition2indicates, the overinvestment factor Δ depends on β, which measures the difference in the size of productivity shocks across firms. Specifically, as a function of β, the overinvestment factor Δ has an inverted U-shape that reaches a maximum atβ̂=233≅1.15and a minimum (i.e.,Δ=1) whenβ≥β*≅1.84. In other words, when differences in productivity are relatively small (i.e., whenβ<β*), the optimal separating mechanism requires the high-prospect firm to overinvest to convey its type to the agent. However, when differences in productivity are large enough (i.e., whenβ≥β*), there is no overinvestment in the optimal separating mechanism. When this is the case, the optimal mechanism corresponds to the optimal mechanism in the benchmark case where the agent can observe θ, that is,Ms*=M*whenβ≥β*. Figure2graphically displays the overinvestment factor Δ as a function of β.
It is worth stressing thatMs*includes overinvestment (and overcompensation) even though it is possible to design separating mechanisms that rely exclusively on distortions in compensation. Investment distortions are included in the optimal mechanism for two reasons. First, investment distortions more efficiently convey information about a firm's prospects because higher investment is costlier for a type 1 firm than for a type β firm. Second, separating mechanisms that rely exclusively on distorting compensation can be costlier for a type β firm than a type 1 firm because effort incentives require that compensation be contingent on success, and the probability of success is higher for a type β firm. In other words, while a higherwβmay help deter the type 1 from mimicking a type β firm, a higherwβis more costly for a type β firm because it is more likely that the type 1 pays the highwβ.
It is also natural to consider whether by shaping hisown compensationthe principal can convey information about the firm's type. For example, the principal might consider separating mechanisms with optimal investment in which information is conveyed by choosing a higher-powered principal's compensation whenθ=βthan whenθ=1. It is straightforward to show that this cannot be the case. Since the program in expressions11to14includes an adding-up constraint (which implies that the principal is the residual claimant to the firm's output net of wages), mechanisms that distort the principal's payoffs are equivalent to mechanisms that distort the agent's compensation. Therefore, our analysis summarized in Proposition2does in fact (implicitly) consider mechanisms in which the principal can shape his own compensation to convey information about the firm's type.
Alternatively, we could have considered mechanisms in which the adding-up constraint is relaxed by allowing the principal to commit to give up a share of the firm's output in some situations (i.e., to burn money).22In the Appendix, we show that, under a mild parametric condition, any mechanism that includes burning money is strictly dominated by the optimal mechanism described in Proposition2. Intuitively, this occurs because any incentive effect produced by burning money can be replicated by a properly designed combination of overinvestment and compensation. This observation implies that the optimal separating mechanism derived in Proposition2is also the optimal mechanism in a richer setting in which the principal can make positive third-party side payments or engage in any other form of money-burning.23
Finally, it is worth noting that our overinvestment result is obtained relative to the benchmark case with symmetric information about θ and incentive problems due to unobservable efforte. We refer to our results as “overinvestment” since the marginal profit obtained from the last unit of invested capital is negative but firms nevertheless invest to convey favorable information to their workers.24

In the optimal pooling mechanismMp*, the level of the agent's effort is the average of the two effort levels under the benchmark and the optimal separating cases, that is,ep*=rθ¯2cand the investment choicekp*does not depend on the realized θ, which implies that the firm overinvests whenθ=1and underinvests whenθ=β. Furthermore, underMp*, compensation is the same proportion of realized output as inM*andMs*, that is,wp*rkp*=12.
The presence of investment rigidities, which is part of an optimally designed investment process, is a notable result that arises due to the interaction between two key ingredients in our setting: (i) the presence of a moral hazard problem, which makes the principal less inclined to disclose information to the worker, and (ii) the inability of the principal to commit to suboptimal choices conditional on the observed θ, which makes the principal unable to rely on richer contracting spaces (e.g., mediated mechanisms) that could create some investment flexibility at the expense of some ex post investment inefficiencies.
We now compareMp*toMs*, that is, we now examine the trade-off between investment rigidities and overinvestment. In particular, while in the optimal separating mechanismMs*the firm overinvests and overcompensates the worker whenθ=β, in the optimal pooling mechanismMp*the cost is that the firm does not tailor its investment expenditures to its realized marginal productivity. The choice betweenMs*andMp*thus depends on the trade-off between these costs and benefits, as the next proposition states.
Proposition 4.Depending on parameters, eitherMs*orMp*is the optimal mechanism. The difference in firm value generated byMs*relative toMp*, that is,(Vs*−Vp*), is (i) decreasing in the likelihood of the high-productivity firm (i.e., decreasing in π) and (ii) increasing in the difference in productivity between firm types (i.e., increasing in β).

Proposition4states that bothMs*andMp*can be optimal mechanisms. WhenMs*is the optimal mechanism, the firm exhibits overinvestment as a part its investment policy. By contrast, whenMp*is optimal, rigidities in investment and compensation are an integral part of the optimal capital budgeting policy. Proposition4is illustrated in Figure3, which displays the regions in the space(β,π)where each type of mechanism is optimal.
Intuitively, the comparative statics results can be described by considering two limiting cases. First, when π is close to one (i.e.,θ=βis highly likely), underMp*the type β investment is close to the full-information investment level while the expected cost of overinvestment for type 1 is very small, whileMs*requires the type β firm to substantially overinvest and thus produces a sizable expected overinvestment cost.25Second, whenβ→β*, the efficiency loss inMp*is large because the average investment is far from the full-information level for both types while the overinvestment inefficiency inherent inMs*is reduced. In fact,Ms*converges toM*since the type 1 firm invests efficiently and the degree of overinvestment of the type β firm goes to zero.
article.III. The Two-Division Firm Case:
Thus far we have established that firms with privately informed executives tend to overinvest relative to the first best (i.e., the separating mechanism), or, because of this tendency, commit to investment rigidities (i.e., the pooling mechanism). In this section we extend our analysis to consider how these incentive issues influence the design of the capital budgeting process in multi-divisional firms. In doing so, we shed light on how the inherent tendency to overinvest to convey favorable prospects can potentially affect how firms are structured.
We consider a two-division extension of the framework examined earlier. Specifically, we analyze the design of the capital budgeting process for a firm that consists of two divisions that are each (ex ante) identical to the single-division firm analyzed previously. In particular, we assume that each divisiond=A,Bemploys one worker whose effort affects the profitability of his division. The firm experiences a productivity shock in each division, that is,Θ=(θA,θB), where ford=A,B,θd∈{1,β}withPr(θd=β)=πand1<β<β*. We assume that the shocks areindependentacross divisions, which implies that the firm's productivity shock can take one of four valuesΘ∈{(1,1),(1,β),(β,1),(β,β)}with respective probabilitiesPr(Θ)={(1−π)2,(1−π)π,π(1−π),π2}.26Our assumptions on commitment and the timing of events are as described in Figure1.
In what follows, we analyze the optimal mechanism for the two-division firm and compare it to the optimal mechanism described earlier, for the case in which each division operates as an independent single-division firm.27
As we show above, in single-division firms the optimal mechanism is always either a pooling mechanism or a separating mechanism. In contrast, the analysis in this subsection shows that in the two-division case the optimal mechanism can involve partial pooling. To demonstrate this result, we derive the optimal pooling and separating mechanisms in the two-division firm and then examine whether partial pooling mechanisms can generate additional firm value relative to these mechanisms.
In the two-division firm, a pooling mechanismM2p={(wAp,kAp,eAp),(wBp,kBp,eBp)}consists of two contracts that represent a commitment to worker compensation, an investment expenditure, and induced efforts in each division that are independent of the division's type. The following lemma describes the optimal pooling mechanismM2p*, which can be obtained by solving a problem analogous to that defined by expressions16and17.
Lemma 1.The optimal pooling mechanism for the two-division firm replicates the optimal pooling mechanisms for two single-division firms, that is,M2p*={(wp*,kp*,ep*),(wp*,kp*,ep*)}.

Intuitively, since the divisions are subject to independent shocks, the investment choice of one division conveys no information about the other division's type. As a result, a two-division firm does not benefit from implementing a pooling mechanism that differs from the mechanisms obtained for two independent single-division firms.
The solution to the previous problem is stated in the following lemma.
Lemma 2.The optimal separating mechanism for the two-division firmM2s*replicates the optimal separating mechanism for two single-division firms. Specifically, the optimal compensation, investment, and effort arewθd*=ws*,kθd*=ks*, andeθd*=es*, respectively, for each divisiond=A,B.

Lemma2states that the optimal separating mechanism for a two-division firm is a perfect replica of the optimal separating mechanisms that arise if each of the divisions acts as an independent single-division firm. In particular, the lemma states that a low-type division invests the first best levelk1*regardless of the other division's type, while a high-type division overinvests at levelkβs*regardless of the other division's type. Therefore, Lemma2also implies that having two divisions with independent shocks does not reduce the cost (i.e., the investment and compensation distortions) of fully conveying information about firm types to the worker. This results means that, relative to the distortions in allocations described inMs*for single-division firms, a two-division firm does not benefit from shifting investment or wages between divisions.
In summary, Lemmas1and2indicate that if the optimal mechanism in the two-division firm generates either complete separation or complete pooling, there are no gains to be realized by combining two firms (with uncorrelated shocks) into a single two-division firm. As we show next, however, the optimal mechanism for the two-division firm can involve partial pooling, and when this is the case, the value of the two-division firm is strictly higher than the sum of the two independent firm values.

There are still distortions inM2Λ*, however, since extreme types (1, 1) and(β,β)pool with either (β, 1) or (1, β) types, that is, there is a certain degree of overinvestment by type (1, 1) and underinvestment by(β,β). As Proposition5indicates,M2Λ*dominatesM2p*.
Proposition 5.In the two-division case, the optimal SPPMM2Λ*generates greater firm value than the optimal pooling mechanismM2p*.

Proposition5implies that it is never optimal for the two-division firm to use a pooling mechanism to allocate capital, and thus that the capital allocation choice of the two-division firm always reveals some information about its prospects. Intuitively this result obtains because, relative to the optimal pooling mechanismM2p*, the optimal SPPMM2Λ*allows types (β, 1) and (1, β) to be separated without incurring any additional efficiency loss, that is, the IC constraints26and27do not bind in the optimal SPPMM2p*. In fact, the optimal investment, compensation, and effort underM2Λ*vis-á-visM2p*are better tailored to the firm's private information. Put differently, the optimal values inM2Λ*are closer than the optimal values ofM2p*to the optimal values inM*.29
The comparison betweenM2Λ*and the optimal separating mechanismM2s*is less clear-cut. As the following proposition states, there exist cases in whichM2Λ*strictly dominatesM2s*and vice versa.
Proposition 6.There are parameters under whichM2Λ*generates more firm value thanM2s*(i.e.,V2s*−V2Λ*<0) and vice versa (i.e.,V2s*−V2Λ*>0). The difference in firm value generated byM2Λ*relative toM2s*, that is,(V2Λ*−V2s*), is larger the smaller the difference in productivity between firm types (i.e., lower β).

Proposition6also implies that there are parameters in the space(π,β)for whichMs*is an optimal mechanism in the single-division firm butM2s*is not the optimal mechanism in the corresponding two-division firm. More formally, if we defineRΛ*as the region in the space(β,π)over whichV2Λ*≥V2s*andRp*as the region in the space(β,π)over whichVp*≥Vs*, it follows thatRp*⊂RΛ*.
The previous proposition also implies that conglomeration can improve firm value in the absence of technological synergies and informational links between divisions. Intuitively, the gain from conglomeration is directly related to the incentives to distort investment that arise when executives have private information about the divisions' prospects. More specifically, as in the single-division case, an executive in the two-division firm has an incentive to overinvest to exaggerate each of the division's prospects. However, in contrast to the single-division case where the pooling mechanism provides the best way for the executive to eliminate his incentive to exaggerate, in the two-division case the executive can communicate some information by using mechanisms with asymmetric capital allocations. When properly designed, asymmetric mechanisms communicate the ranking of the two divisions' prospects without incurring overinvestment costs.
More specifically, as we show in Proposition3, in the two-division case a pooling mechanism is dominated by an SPPM, that is, a two-contract menu mechanism in which each contract asymmetrically allocates the same amount of total capital. By fixing the total amount of capital, the SPPM eliminates overinvestment, that is, the incentive to exaggerate, while communicating some information, that is, separating type (1, β) firms from type (β, 1). Put differently, relative to a pooling mechanism, a slight asymmetry in capital allocation produces sizable additional benefits due to the separation of types (1, β) and (β, 1) at negligible incremental cost. This intuition is related to insights obtained in multi-dimensional cheap talk settings (e.g., Chakraborty and Harbaugh (2007)) in which the informed party has information about multiple items and can credibly communicate information that he could not provide about a single item in isolation by disclosing a ranking.
Proposition 7.There is a sizable region of parameters in the space(β,π)over which the optimal mechanismM2g*is the optimal partial pooling mechanismM2pp*.
Proposition7implies that, for conglomerates, investment rigidities and limited revelation of information are an integral part of the optimal mechanism for a sizable region of parameters. In particular, if we defineRg*as the region in the space(β,π)over which there are investment rigidities and limited revelation of information in two-division firms andRp*as the region in the space(β,π)over which there are rigidities and limited revelation of information in single-division firms, we obtainRp*⊂Rg*, that is, some form of rigidities are more prevalent in conglomerates.
Note that Proposition7does not state the nature of the optimal contract for all possible combinations of parameters in the space(β,π). Although our analysis does not exclude the possibility that complete separation is the optimal mechanism for some parameters, we have not been able to analytically prove that this is indeed the case. We have, however, run a large set of numerical simulations and found that in each simulation the optimal mechanism is separating whenβ→β*.
We conclude the analysis of the two-division firm by characterizingM2pp*, the optimal partial pooling mechanism whenM2pp*is indeed optimal, that is,M2pp*=M2g*.

While the formal proof of Proposition8shown in the Appendix requires a large number of technical derivations, its logic can be summarized with the following two insights. First, the full separation of types (1, β) and (β, 1) follows an argument similar to that made to obtainM2Λ*. Since, for a given amount of capital, it is costless to separate types (1, β) and (β, 1), mechanisms that do not fully separate (1, β) and (β, 1) are suboptimal. Second, it can be shown thatM2s*dominates any other mechanism in which a type (1, 1) firm is revealed with positive probability. Intuitively, this is because a type (1, 1) firm is revealed with positive probability only when there is some degree of overinvestment by the other types' contracts. Indeed, what characterizesM2s*is the fact that it is the most efficient mechanism among those in which only type β divisions overinvest. As a result, a mechanism in which a type (1, 1) firm is revealed with positive probability is dominated byM2s*.
In summary, as established in Proposition8, a two-division firm's capital allocation is characterized by some degree of information transmission, that is, types (1, β) and (β, 1) are fully separated. In addition, for cases in which full separation does not occur, there is a specific type of distortion: the type (1, 1) firm pools with more productive types and thus overinvests relative to the case with full information. In SectionV.we discuss the empirical relevance of these results.
article.IV. Extensions:
Our analysis can be extended in a number of directions. First, it can be extended to a setting with a continuum of types where the productivity shock θ can take any value in the interval[1,β¯].30In this setting, we can show that the tendency toward overinvestment remains and that investment rigidities (i.e., pooling) are present in the optimal mechanism. An interesting property of the optimal mechanism in the continuum-of-types setting is that there is both “pooling at the bottom” (i.e., fixed and identical-across-types investment expenditure and compensationk̲andw̲are chosen by a positive measure of types at the bottom of the type distribution, that is,[1,β′]) and “pooling at the top” (that is, fixed and identical-across-types investment expenditurek¯andw¯are chosen by a positive measure of types at the top of the type distribution, that is,[β′′,β¯]). Since it can be shown thatk̲⩽k¯, the findings that arise in the continuum-of-types setting are consistent with the idea that firms require a positive NPV threshold before investing, but place a cap on the maximum level of investment.
Our analysis can also be extended to examine how the participation of uninformed third parties can alter optimal investment rules.31We have considered two roles that third parties can play. The first is the role of the mediator, as described in Myerson (1983), who receives private communications and then privately and confidentially recommends specific actions to the parties involved. In this case, as discussed in SectionB.1., if a mediator restores the principal's ability to commit in advance to investment plans that are ex post inefficient, then the mediator's presence leads to changes in investment allocations that improve firm value.32In particular, it can be shown that the optimal mechanism under full commitment is as follows. The informed principal truthfully discloses his information to a mediator, but not to the agent. Conditional on the information communicated by the principal, the mediator produces a garbled signal, observed by the agent, which determines the allocation (i.e., investment levelkand agent compensationw) that is carried out by the principal. Thus, when it is feasible to use a mediator in this way, investment rules will be less rigid but will still convey a limited amount of information to the firm's stakeholders.
The second potential role of a third party is that of a budget breaker, that is, a party who is uninvolved in the production or decision process but who has a claim on the firm's output. A budget breaker can broaden the contract space and alter the principal's incentives in ways that lead to more efficient investment choices.33Specifically, the presence of the third party can alter the IC constraints of the principal in a way that allows him to commit to future actions that would not be incentive compatible without the third party.
Debtholders provide one example of a third-party budget breaker that may be relevant. In particular, we have also analyzed a slightly modified setting in which the principal (before becoming informed) issues risky debt to finance the firm but still retains control over the firm's investment and worker compensation. In this modified setting, it can be shown that the principal's incentives to overinvest are mitigated, leading to an optimal investment rule with fewer investment rigidities. More specifically, the separating mechanism becomes relatively more attractive since the firm's debt obligations affect the principal's IC constraints by reducing the benefits of low-prospect firms to mimic high-prospect firms. Intuitively, the presence of debt overhang decreases the low type's benefits from overinvesting more than it decreases the high type's benefits, which reduces the amount the high type must overinvest to separate.
The effect of debt on the design of optimal investment rules in this setting illustrates that firms that are run in the best interest of their owners can find it useful to take on debt to curb overinvestment incentives. Our results therefore illustrate how it is possible to obtain similar conclusions to those in Jensen (1986), Stulz (1990), and Hart and Moore (1995) in a model in which firms are subject to information asymmetry rather than managerial agency conflicts.
article.V. Empirical Implications:
The empirical relevance of our analysis can be examined from several perspectives. First, our analysis implies the prevalence of certain investment distortions, such as rigidities and overinvestment, in firms' capital investment process. Consistent with this observation, Cooper and Haltiwanger (2006) present evidence that firms have minimum investment thresholds and that large bursts of investment expenditures account for about 50% of total investment activity.34Moreover, in a survey of the capital allocation rules of manufacturing firms, Ross (1986) documents that, in addition to these minimum thresholds, these firms impose caps on their expenditures.
In addition, our analysis suggests that a study of investment distortions should consider firms along a number of different dimensions. In particular, our model predicts more investment distortions in more opaque firms, where key employees and other stakeholders are less informed than top executives about the firm's future outlook, and in firms whose stakeholders must take unobservable actions that are crucial to the firm's success. Furthermore, if stakeholder relationships are particularly important for the development and survival of entrepreneurial ventures and newly created firms (as discussed in Bhide and Stevenson (1999)), we expect that new firms distort their investment allocations more than mature firms. Similarly, our results are likely to be more relevant for firms that are developing new technologies, firms in which information asymmetries are inherently important and hence the incentives to convey information to stakeholders are strong.
Our model also makes predictions about the type of distortion (i.e., overinvestment versus investment rigidities) that is most likely to arise. According to the comparative statics results reported in Proposition4, investment rigidities (i.e., pooling) are likely to arise when differences between types are lower (low β). Intuitively, if one takes the two-type case as a useful abstraction of a reality characterized by a continuum of types (and thus the existence of types that are relatively close), this result suggests that rigidities are likely to be observed frequently. Proposition4also indicates that investment rigidities are more likely to arise when the ex ante likelihood of a high productivity project (i.e., high π) is higher. We expect this to be the case for firms in industries with favorable growth opportunities and, more generally, during economic expansions.35
Our model further suggests substantial differences between investment distortions in single-division and multi-division firms. Although there would be obvious endogeneity challenges in studying such differences, a potential way to do so would be to examine changes in firms' investment distortions before and after mergers and spin-offs. Our analysis suggests that conglomerates should have capital budgeting processes that exhibit more abundant but less stringent distortions (i.e., partial pooling mechanisms are more likely to be optimal in conglomerates). More specifically, our analysis predicts that, after merging, firms (i) should eliminate some rigidities in investment rules if they formerly employed very rigid investment rules as single-division firms (i.e., pooling), but (ii) should introduce some rigidities if they formerly employed very few rigidities (i.e., separation). Our analysis also suggests that multi-divisional firms would tend to avoid extreme capital allocations.
This last implication, that is, the avoidance of extreme capital allocations, is consistent with evidence in the conglomerates literature of a certain degree of socialism in capital allocation.36In particular, this literature stresses the tendency of better- (lower-) performing divisions in conglomerates being allocated less (more) capital than their single-division counterparts and associates this behavior with potential agency problems such as managerial empire-building or rent-seeking behavior (see, for example, Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000)). As Proposition7suggests, when executives possess private information about firm prospects, the optimal investment policy tends toward overinvestment when both divisions exhibit unfavorable prospects. It is worth mentioning that, while the literature on investment distortions in conglomerates relies on managerial agency problems, the lack of sensitivity of investment to information arises in our setting even though firms are run in their owners' best interests.
To test the above predictions, two major challenges will need to be overcome. The first challenge relates to obtaining data on investment distortions. While investment expenditures are observable at the firm level as well as the individual plant level (e.g., Cooper and Haltiwanger (2006)), one cannot observe what these expenditures would be absent informational asymmetries. To address this issue, one might survey corporate executives about the extent to which they impose constraints on future investment choices.37The second challenge relates to endogeneity issues. We expect firms to merge and spin off divisions for reasons that relate to the investment process and when doing so create value. This creates a potential opportunity as well as a challenge. Since our analysis produces testable predictions on the gains and costs of conglomeration, an analysis of mergers and spin-offs could provide another lens through which to evaluate our model.
article.VI. Concluding Remarks:
Since the 1970s financial economists have studied the transmission of information from firms to financial markets. In this paper we examine the transmission of information within an organization, that is, between a firm's top executives who set the firm's overall strategy, and the firm's lower-level employees, whose productive efforts are required to realize this strategy. In our model, strategic choices convey information, and thus may distort executives' choices. Specifically, our top-down model of capital allocation suggests that an unconstrained executive tends to overinvest, since higher levels of investment convey more favorable information, which induces the firm's employees to work harder.
Of course, rational workers understand the firm's incentive to overinvest and can back out relevant information from observed capital expenditures. As our model shows, the optimal mechanism for allocating capital is affected by the costs and benefits associated with communicating information to workers. In some situations the benefits of a flexible investment policy—one that communicates information fully to workers—exceed the overinvestment costs, while in other situations rules that limit overinvestment, and thus reduce the transmission of information to workers, can result in higher firm value.
Allocations can also be improved by expanding the scope of the firm, that is, by organizing activities in multi-division conglomerates rather than as independent single-division firms. Indeed, as we show, even when the prospects of the two entities are completely independent, firm value can be increased by managing them as a single firm. The added value comes from an enhanced ability to convey information about the firm's prospects (i.e., about the prospects of the individual divisions) without necessarily resorting to overinvestment or the implementation of rigid investment rules.
The idea that firms can influence the transmission of information to stakeholders through their organizational design has a number of implications. For example, rather than putting constraints on investment choices, the firm may obfuscate those choices, which would reduce incentives to overinvest. It may also be possible to improve firm value by delegating aspects of the investment decision process to parties that are less informed than the executive. For example, third parties such as uninformed managers or consultants can act as mediators who muddle the information conveyed by investment expenditures, or act as budget breakers who alter the executives' payoffs and lead them to a reduction in investment distortions.
Finally, one can look beyond employees' effort choices and consider how firms' choices are influenced by their incentives to retain and attract not only employees but also other stakeholders, such as customers and suppliers, whose perceptions are also key to firm success. An important difference between these stakeholders and the workers considered in our model is that contracting on the contributions of these stakeholders is likely to be more challenging. While it seems reasonable to expect that our intuition about investment distortions would continue to hold in a setting with additional contracting imperfections, we leave an analysis of these issues to future research.
